chunk_summary: The five stages of customer awareness (from Eugene Schwarz, 1960s): 1) Unaware — doesn't know they have a problem. 2) Problem Aware — knows they have a problem but not solutions. 3) Solution Aware — knows solutions exist but not specific products. 4) Product Aware — knows your product but still comparing. 5) Most Aware — ready to buy or already a customer. Each stage maps to different search keywords and content needs.
content: Eugene Schwarz in the 1950 60s said that there are five stages of customer awareness and he was an ad Mogul ... he wrote very much about consumer awareness selling Gillette razors or selling you know paper towels or something but they apply pretty well to B2B as well so the first stage top down five stages first stage is people who are unaware so your prospect doesn't realize they have a problem they don't know there's a way to improve their current struggle you can imagine this B2B unaware is actually kind of dangerous if people are unaware they even have a problem often times you rather inventing a category or it's all outbound and it's education ... second one is problem aware your prospect knows they have a problem they're unfamiliar with possible solutions they may not completely understand the problem so this is when they realize oh I need more I have a problem I don't have enough buyers and sellers so how do I go about doing that do I go to Google do I ask in Facebook groups how are other people finding it ... the thing is they're not searching for MailChimp because they don't even know what that is in fact they're not even searching for email marketing software because they don't know what that is ... solution aware is stage three this is where your prospect knows about Solutions like yours but they don't know about specific products so this might be where they realize oh there is a category marketing automation email marketing ... and then product aware this is where they start learning they may know of your product they're still comparing it to the competition they're not sure if your product is the best solution for them ... and then we get to the point of most aware this is where someone has really educated themselves on the market they're probably close to making a purchase maybe they've even made a purchase and now they're telling their friends
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the five stages of customer awareness?
How does the awareness framework apply to B2B?
What is the difference between solution aware and product aware?
Why is the unaware stage dangerous in B2B?

chunk_summary: Rob's failure story: In 2012-13 he spent $5-6K on content marketing for Drip (then VelvetMail) before launch. The content was all top-of-funnel (email marketing myths, beginner guides) with no framework. It got crickets. The lesson: without the awareness-stage framework, you waste money on content that's too general and too far from purchase intent.
content: I want to tell you a story 2012 2013 Mr Derek Rymer and I are breaking ground on this code Base that later became drip at the time it was velvet mail velvet mail.com baby this was before chat GPT could help me name things so I was like all right Derek you write code and stuff and I'm going to go start the marketing engine because you're supposed to start marketing the day start coding which we did and so I believe I forget the number but it's like five thousand $6,000 I paid a writer to create a bunch of content because we were going to do content marketing again this is 2012 13 this is 11 12 years ago before I really knew what I was doing and it was a lot of money right I was bootstrapping this thing so to spend five grand before we launched and then to launch all this content over you know the first six months it was out and then it was basically crickets the content marketing effort did not work and the reason it did not work in retrospect is because I didn't have any type of plan or strategy or framework to how to prioritize what content should we do because if you look at the names of these things seven email marketing myths a beginners guide to email marketing five things to know about email marketing they're very general they're very top of funnel and in this talk I'm going to talk about how you would do it you should do it differently than I did
questions_on_this_chunk: What mistake did Rob make with Drip's content marketing?
Why did early Drip content fail?
Should you start content marketing before launch?
Why is top-of-funnel content alone insufficient?

chunk_summary: The key strategy: start content marketing from the BOTTOM of the funnel (stages 4-5: product aware and most aware) and work backwards. This content serves double duty — it helps both marketing AND sales (case studies, vs pages, feature breakdowns help close deals). Once you have bottom-funnel content converting, expand to middle (solution aware) and then top (problem/unaware).
content: originally when I wrote this talk you should start at the bottom is what you should do you should start at stage five at the most aware stage all right problem is is if the I wrote the whole next section of starting from the bottom and going up and it was like watching momento if good movie very confusing because everything's backwards so I'm actually going to start it unaware because it just makes sense to do the progression but you'll get the slides and you'll get to see like really if you have nothing if you have none of this I would start at that most aware stage creating the content examples I give there and I would work my way backwards
questions_on_this_chunk: Which stage of awareness should I start content for?

chunk_summary: Content for Unaware & Problem Aware (stages 1-2): high-level educational content — YouTube courses, templates, how-to guides that don't mention your product. Examples: SignWell's free contract templates, VEED's 'add subtitles to video' SEO pages, SessionLab's workshop templates, Userlist's 'what is user onboarding' guides. These cast a wide net but take a long time to convert.
content: so unaware y'all are familiar with this this is the hardest one with B2B because again it's just not something like if someone's completely unaware that they even need email marketing how do I how do you even market to that you can do high level stuff so YouTube videos 10 email marketing tips email marketing full course ... session lab is a European startup and they help folks organize Workshop schedules you can see they have really high level Stuff how to use room setup Styles how to become a great Workshop facilitator online energizers more engaging meetings I mean they're really going they're about Workshop agendas and this is the next higher level up I'm sure they have a lot of search traffic to these but they're so high in the funnel that it's going to take folks a long long time to get down that's why I say start at the bottom ... problem aware folks are aware they have a problem this is where in my example earlier of your realtor looking for email marketing might search for how do I send bulk email what is email marketing how do I do email marketing email marketing templates ... Sewell is really good he the bulk of Sewell's traffic is organic Google organic he's great at putting out contract templates and agreements because this from 25,000 sales documents this is an amazing stage two approach because it is when people know they have a problem and they don't know that they need electronic signature yet but the problem they're trying to solve just now is they need a template so he gives it away ... Ved v.o the co-founder of Ved they do a really good job with SEO tools add image to video add music to video add text to video add subtitles to video people are aware they have a problem they don't know that they need Ved's video editing software ... user list many folks here familiar with Benedict and Jane what is user onboarding The Ultimate Guide for SAS companies again it's something that's higher up in the funnel

chunk_summary: Content for Solution Aware & Product Aware (stages 3-4): 'best X software' pages, alternative-to pages, and versus pages. Monitor Reddit, Hacker News, and communities with F5Bot or Syften for people mentioning your product or category. Capterra and G2 are huge for this stage — people on these sites are deep in the funnel. Run ads on Capterra for high-intent buyers. Answer comparison questions proactively.
content: solution aware they know about Solutions but they don't know about specific products so this is where that Realtor might search for email marketing software best email marketing software what is email marketing software what's interesting is Google is still a great source for this often they wind up on Capa G2 Reddit and Kora so Capa and G2 depending on the way to figure this out is you go into an incognito browser search for best whatever your category is top whatever your category is and see which ranks higher Capa G2 ... hopefully it's Capa because frankly they're the easiest one to work with an advertise on but you can buy ads and Capa and they're highly highly qualified folks that are already kind of become they're people who hit Capa are usually at stage three or four so they're deeper in the funnel ... red people often wind up on Reddit and Kora as well I personally for like microcom and Tiny Seed I monitor these things I use two tools for this there's F5 bot and then Syften ... I get pinged with an email or in slack and it's like hey someone's talking about you or your book or microcom or TinySeed obviously you would put your own SAS product in there you could pop in you can weigh in or not of course but these people are usually asking what do you think of product they are product aware ... so if people mention your product name they're product aware by definition so now I have a stage four person and a conversation around my company ... product aware when they know the name of your product ... this is where you're going to think about alternative to pages and versus pages ... alternative to pages work when you are trying to piggyback on the SEO or the brand value of that competitor so are people searching for alternative to HubSpot clavio active campaign MailChimp well yes so then you put those pages in

chunk_summary: Content for Most Aware (stage 5): detailed feature pages (not just benefits), long-form demos (45 min), knowledge base/documentation, detailed versus pages, case studies, white papers, and security/compliance pages. Example: SignWell's security page answers compliance questions that only matter right before purchase. At this stage, you must go deeper than benefits and show exactly how the product works.
content: last stage is the most aware this when people are really approaching making a decision they're looking comparisons alternative twos and all that this is where you have to break the Paradigm you have to get into features and not just talk about benefits at some point you know it's always benefits not features it's not always it's benefits not features when you're marketing when someone's first finding out about you but at a certain point they need to go a layer deeper the deeper into the stages they go they need to figure out what are your actual features ... your knowledge base being decent at this point people are looking at that thinking do I want to buy from this company is their documentation good versus Pages I've said long form demos this is where you go from that 60-second demo on your homepage to having a 45 minute demo someone can watch now maybe it's one-on-one but maybe it's pre-recorded if I'm ready to buy software I will 100% watch a 30 minute or 45 minute demo of someone going through it at 2x of course case studies I talked about and white papers ... drip actually does a good job I didn't do any of this they did this after I left but drip does a good job of showing you their features not benefits as you dig in ... user list does a great job of this you can see boom you can dig in and see exactly what they're doing so even on their marketing site there's features KB can be super helpful I love user lists KB with all their Integrations listed ... I love what Ruben did with this on Sewell not something I had thought of but I bet all of y'all get asked a lot about security and compliance and this alone this piece of content marketing talk about a stage five piece if you're about to buy are you worried about compliance at sock 2 Hippa gdpr Network and security system reliability data of course you are do you care at all about that when you're at stage one or two you don't so this is where bucketing content into these stages can help you build the most appropriate stuff for the bottom of the funnel and work your way back up again

chunk_summary: How to identify what stage a prospect is in: 1) On sales calls, ask when they plan to buy and which products they're comparing to. 2) Look at keywords — informational, commercial (best, top, review, vs), and transactional (buy, discount, cheap) map loosely to stages. 3) Use SEO tools like Semrush and SimilarWeb which now show keyword intent (I=informational, C=commercial, T=transactional, N=navigational). It's not 100% accurate but good enough to prioritize.
content: how to figure out where people are approximately ... there's three ways that I've seen people figure out okay if I have an individual I'm talking to on a sales call or if I have kind of a cohort or a group ... the first way is why you should ask on your sales calls when do you plan to make a purchase are you comparing us to other products which other products this will tell you a lot ... if I'm going to make a purchase very soon well I'm probably at the fourth third fourth stage of awareness if I'm comparing you to mailchip and clavio we're probably at the fourth or the fifth stage ... the second way is to look at actual keywords ... this is from semrush and they talk about how there are navigational informational commercial and transactional and these Loosely apply ... informational is probably a stage two maybe stage three commercial investigation certainly is stage three and four transaction will become stage five ... you can look at specific keywords like best top review you will see best email service providers best email marketing software best electronic signature software top 10 electronic signature software review of XYZ signature software comparison compare MailChimp versus clavio ... and then transactional is when they get right down to purchase buy discount coupon cheap ... and then there are two tools that do this and I didn't realize until I started researching this that if you look at semrush their dashboard they actually have an intent and the C is for commercial the T is for transactional the n is for navigational and the I is for informational they actually will tell you based on the best of their ability and all their AI that's running in the background what each keyword means ... similarweb is the other piece of software should have highlighted this ... that says intent has nav transact local so it is a relatively standardized thing
